I now have sufficient information from all 12 papers. Let me compile the final analysis.

Here is the complete analysis for all 12 papers:

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC11003701.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Sertraline (CYP2B6 noted as a contributor to sertraline metabolism, though CYP2B6 genotype data were not available in this study's dataset; the paper references the CPIC guideline for CYP2B6 and serotonin reuptake inhibitors)
PHENOTYPES: Anxiety disorders, depressive disorders (pediatric psychiatric hospitalization population)
NOTES: This paper focuses primarily on CYP2C19 and CYP2D6 genotype effects on escitalopram and sertraline pharmacokinetics in pediatric patients. CYP2B6 is mentioned explicitly as a contributor to sertraline metabolism, and the paper cites the CPIC guideline for CYP2B6/serotonin reuptake inhibitor antidepressants, but no specific CYP2B6 alleles (*1, *4, *6, *9, *18, *28) are directly characterized or reported. The paper does note that CYP2B6 genotypes were unavailable for this cohort. Relevance is partial/indirect.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC11958640.md
RELEVANT: no
DRUGS: None relevant (focuses on glucocorticoids and asthma via AHR/eRNA signatures)
PHENOTYPES: Asthma
NOTES: This paper is about enhancer RNA transcription, SNPs linked to asthma risk, and the aryl hydrocarbon receptor pathway. CYP2B6 and its variant alleles (*1, *4, *6, *9, *18, *28) are not discussed anywhere in this paper.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC2000724.md
RELEVANT: no
DRUGS: Duloxetine (metabolized by CYP1A2 and CYP2D6, not CYP2B6)
PHENOTYPES: Depression, diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain, stress urinary incontinence
NOTES: This paper compares duloxetine pharmacokinetics in Japanese vs. Caucasian subjects. Duloxetine metabolism involves CYP1A2 and CYP2D6; CYP2B6 and its variants are not mentioned anywhere in the paper.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC2662939.md
RELEVANT: no
DRUGS: Montelukast (leukotriene pathway modifier)
PHENOTYPES: Asthma
NOTES: This paper investigates leukotriene pathway gene polymorphisms and montelukast response in asthma patients. CYP2B6 and any of its variants (*1, *4, *6, *9, *18, *28) are not discussed.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC2679106.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Efavirenz
PHENOTYPES: HIV infection, HIV/tuberculosis co-infection; associated clinical outcomes include virological failure and central nervous system (CNS) adverse effects
NOTES: This paper directly studies CYP2B6 c.516G>T (the defining SNP of *6, also present in *9 via Q172H) and c.983T>C (defining SNP of *18) in Ghanaian HIV-infected patients. The *6 allele (via the 516G>T SNP, Q172H) is the primary focus; the 983T>C SNP is also genotyped (associated with *18). The 516TT genotype was associated with >5-fold higher median efavirenz concentrations. The paper does not use the specific allele names *1, *4, *9, *18, or *28 in its body but the SNPs studied directly map to those alleles.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC2754599.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Efavirenz (also mentions AZT/3TC as co-administered drugs)
PHENOTYPES: HIV-1 infection; clinical phenotypes include altered plasma efavirenz exposure, virological failure, and implicitly CNS toxicity risk
NOTES: This paper comprehensively screens 50 SNPs in CYP2B6 in Haitian HIV-infected patients and maps associations to efavirenz plasma concentrations. It primarily reports CYP2B6 516G>T (marker of *6 and *9) as the dominant predictor of increased efavirenz exposure (Spearman's rho = 0.71). It also identifies novel polymorphisms associated with decreased efavirenz exposure. The composite 516/983 genotype is used to define extensive, intermediate, and slow metabolizer phenotypes. CYP2B6 *18 (via 983T>C) is implicitly encompassed. The *1 wild-type is the reference. Specific star allele names *6, *9, and *18 are referenced through their defining SNPs throughout.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC2791975.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Efavirenz
PHENOTYPES: HIV/AIDS; clinical phenotypes include altered efavirenz plasma exposure (AUC, clearance), with implications for CNS toxicity and virological response
NOTES: This paper directly names and tests CYP2B6*6 (G516T and A785G, in complete linkage disequilibrium) and CYP2B6*18 (c.983T>C, listed in genotyped SNP table as associated with *16 and *18; tested but not significant in the final model). CYP2B6*6 homozygotes showed 21% lower apparent oral clearance of efavirenz compared to extensive metabolizers (wild-type/*1). CYP2B6*18 was included in the univariate screen but did not reach significance in the final multivariate model. CYP2B6*11 was the other significant allele identified. This paper directly names *6 and *18 among the alleles evaluated.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC2875867.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Efavirenz
PHENOTYPES: HIV infection (HIV/TB co-infection subgroup also included); clinical outcomes include CNS side effects and virological failure risk
NOTES: This paper genotypes CYP2B6 c.516G>T (Q172H, marker of *6/*9) and c.983T>C (I328T, marker of *18) in Ghanaian HIV patients. The CYP2B6 516TT genotype was the strongest predictor of elevated efavirenz concentrations (45.2% of total variance). CYP2B6 *9 carrier status is explicitly tested as a separate variable and found not significant, while CYP2B6 *17 carrier status (CYP2A6) and UGT2B7 were also studied. The paper discusses CYP2B6 *6 (homozygous) associated with CNS toxicity and requirement for dose reduction. Directly names and tests *6, *9 (via CYP2B6 516G>T), and *18 (via 983T>C).

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC3164347.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Bupropion, efavirenz, 7-ethoxy-4-trifluoromethylcoumarin (7-EFC, a probe substrate); cyclophosphamide and propofol are mentioned as other CYP2B6 substrates
PHENOTYPES: This is an in vitro enzymology paper; no clinical disease phenotypes are the primary focus, but the implications for neurotoxicity (elevated efavirenz plasma levels in *6 carriers) and altered drug metabolism are discussed
NOTES: This is a detailed in vitro functional characterization paper. It directly studies CYP2B6.1 (i.e., *1, wild-type), CYP2B6.4 (*4, K262R single mutant), CYP2B6.5 (*5, R487C single mutant), CYP2B6.6 (*6, Q172H/K262R double mutant), CYP2B6.7 (*7, Q172H/K262R/R487C triple mutant), CYP2B6.8 (*8, K139E single mutant), and CYP2B6.9 (*9, Q172H single mutant). All of *1, *4, *6, and *9 from the query list are directly characterized. *18 and *28 are not studied in this paper. Key findings: *6 shows 4- and 27-fold increases in Km for bupropion and efavirenz respectively (substantially reduced catalytic efficiency); *9 shows decreased catalytic efficiency for both bupropion and efavirenz; *4 shows decreased catalytic efficiency; *8 is a complete loss-of-function variant unable to accept electrons from CPR.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC3195031.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Efavirenz
PHENOTYPES: HIV infection; clinical phenotypes include CNS/neuropsychiatric adverse effects (dizziness, abnormal dreams, hallucinations, psychosis), virological failure, and efavirenz intoxication
NOTES: This paper explicitly identifies CYP2B6*6 (defined by 516G>T and 785A>G together) as the primary genetic determinant of efavirenz pharmacokinetics in Caucasian HIV patients. The model stratifies patients by *6 genotype: G/G (wild-type/*1), G/T (heterozygous), and T/T (homozygous *6). Heterozygous *6 reduces CL/F by approximately 40% and homozygous *6 reduces CL/F by approximately 65% vs. wild-type. The paper also references *1 (wild-type reference) and *6, and discusses dose reduction recommendations (600 mg/day for G/G; 400 mg/day for G/T; 200 mg/day for T/T). Other variants (*4, *9, *18, *28) were genotyped but were absent or had low frequency in the Caucasian study population.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC3466997.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Efavirenz
PHENOTYPES: HIV infection in women; clinical phenotypes include elevated drug exposure (toxicity risk), CNS/neuropsychiatric adverse effects, treatment discontinuation, and virological failure
NOTES: This paper studies 182 SNPs and 45 haplotypes in HIV-infected women. CYP2B6 516G>T (the primary marker of *6 and *9) is the dominant predictor: homozygous TT genotype gives >3-fold increases in both AUC (short-term) and hair concentrations (long-term efavirenz exposure). CYP2B6 983T>C (*18 marker) also significantly affects AUC. The paper uses the slow metabolizer classification based on 516TT or 983CC (or compound heterozygotes), referencing *6/*9 and *18 alleles through their SNPs. Individuals with 516TT are described as "slow metabolizers." The paper refers to *6 explicitly in cited literature and characterizes the durable nature of its effect on exposure.

---

PAPER: /app/papers/PMC3522814.md
RELEVANT: yes
DRUGS: Efavirenz
PHENOTYPES: HIV-1 infection; clinical phenotypes include elevated efavirenz plasma concentrations associated with CNS side effects (headache, dizziness, insomnia, hallucinations, acute mania, psychosis) and virological failure risk
NOTES: This paper genotypes nine CYP2B6 SNPs in Thai HIV patients. The key SNPs studied are c.516G>T (marker of *6 and *9) and c.785A>G (marker of *4, *6, *7, and related alleles). Both are significantly associated with higher efavirenz plasma concentrations. The paper explicitly names *6 (c.516G>T and c.785A>G) as the most studied allele, and references *9, *16, *26, *27, and *28 as carriers associated with slower efavirenz oral clearance (cited from literature). The GAC haplotype (516G>T, 785A>G, g.21563C>T) is identified as associated with higher efavirenz concentrations. The paper explicitly names *28 as one of the alleles associated with slower efavirenz clearance in the introduction (citing Gatanaga et al. and Tsuchiya et al.). *1 serves as the wild-type reference throughout.
